Title: Social Media Marketing: A 2026 Strategy Guide | 2POINT
URL: https://www.2pointagency.com/guides/social-media-marketing-the-complete-2026-strategy-guide/
Published: 2026-06-03T14:33:49.000Z
Author: N/A
Highlights:
The most important takeaway from these figures is that social media marketing investment is compounding, not plateauing, making early and deliberate platform strategy a competitive necessity.
...
With 5.
...
## Platform Selection and Social Media Performance Benchmarks
...
Choosing the right platforms is one of the most consequential decisions in any social media marketing strategy. Spreading resources across every available platform is a recipe for mediocrity. The most effective approach focuses investment on the two or three platforms where your target audience is most active, most engaged, and most likely to take the actions your business needs.
...
Platform selection should be driven by audience location, purchase intent, and content format fit rather than platform popularity alone.
...
## Content Strategy and Creation Best Practices for Social Media Marketing
...
Content is the fuel that powers every social media marketing strategy. Without a coherent, platform-aware content approach, even the best audience targeting and advertising spend will underperform. The brands that consistently win on social media are not necessarily the ones with the largest budgets; they are the ones with the clearest content strategy and the discipline to execute it consistently.
...
A sustainable social media content strategy rests on three pillars: educate and inform, entertain and engage, and promote and convert. Each pillar serves a distinct purpose, and the balance between them directly affects both reach and revenue. Most successful brands maintain a 70/30 split, with 70% of content providing genuine value and 30% focused on direct promotion.
...
### Platform-Specific Content Adaptation for Maximum Engagement
...
One of the most common content strategy errors is treating social media as a single channel and publishing identical content across every platform. Each platform has its own visual language, community norms, algorithm behavior, and audience expectations. Content that thrives on LinkedIn will often underperform on TikTok, and vice versa. Platform-specific adaptation is not extra work; it is the work.
...
TikTok and Instagram Reels demand vertical 9:16 formats, trending audio integration, fast-paced editing, and content that hooks viewers within the first two seconds. LinkedIn rewards professional insight, industry analysis, and longer text posts that demonstrate expertise and generate thoughtful comments. Facebook performs best with community-oriented content, conversational captions, and a mix of formats that reflect the platform's broader demographic range. Pinterest requires high-quality visual imagery, informative overlays, and content that functions as both inspiration and practical resource simultaneously.
...
User-generated content (UGC) has become one of the most strategically valuable content types in social media marketing, and its importance is only accelerating. In certain verticals, UGC sees 8.7 times higher engagement than brand-produced content. The reason is straightforward: audiences trust other consumers more than they trust brands, and UGC provides the authentic peer validation that polished brand content simply cannot replicate.
...
## Building a Social Media Marketing Strategy: A Step-by-Step Framework
...
A successful social media marketing strategy does not emerge from platform-by-platform tactical decisions. It begins with clear business objectives and works backward to the platforms, content types, audience strategies, and measurement frameworks that best serve those objectives. The following framework provides a structured approach to building a strategy that is coherent, executable, and measurable.
...
Every social media strategy must begin with business objectives that exist independently of social media. Are you seeking to increase brand awareness in a new market? Generate qualified leads for a sales team? Drive direct e-commerce sales? Reduce customer service costs by deflecting inquiries to social? Retain existing customers by building community? Each objective leads to different platform choices, content strategies, advertising approaches, and measurement frameworks. Attempting to accomplish all objectives simultaneously with a single undifferentiated strategy is the most common reason social media programs fail to deliver clear ROI.
...
### Step 2: Conduct Audience Research and Platform Selection
...
Audience research for social media strategy should answer three core questions: where is the target audience most active, what content do they engage with most, and what role does social media play in their path to purchase? Platform usage data, social listening insights, competitor analysis, and direct customer surveys all contribute to answering these questions with evidence rather than assumption.
...
Platform selection should follow audience research, not precede it. The decision to invest in TikTok, LinkedIn, Instagram, or any other platform should be grounded in evidence that the target audience is meaningfully present and reachable there. The practical implication is focus: committing fully to two or three platforms where the audience is concentrated and engaged consistently outperforms spreading resources thinly across every available platform. The impact of social media on brand loyalty is strongest when brands maintain a consistent, high-quality presence on a small number of relevant platforms rather than a diluted presence everywhere.
...
### Step 3: Develop Content Strategy and Editorial Calendar
...
Content strategy development translates business objectives and audience insights into a repeatable content production system. This includes defining content pillars (the categories of value the brand will consistently provide), formats (video, carousel, long-form text, Stories, etc.), posting cadence by platform, brand voice guidelines, and visual identity standards. A documented content strategy reduces production time, ensures consistency, and provides the framework for evaluating whether individual pieces of content align with strategic goals.
...
The editorial calendar operationalizes the content strategy by scheduling specific content topics, formats, and publication dates across platforms and time periods. Effective editorial calendars balance planned content with space for reactive content that responds to trends, news, and cultural moments. Maintaining brand consistency in marketing across platforms and content types requires the kind of documented guidance that an editorial calendar and style guide provide, ensuring that content created by different team members or at different times maintains a coherent brand voice and visual identity.
...
### Step 4: Execute, Test, and Optimize Continuously
...
Execution discipline separates strategies that remain documents from strategies that drive business results. This means consistent content publication at the planned cadence, active community management that responds to comments and messages within defined timeframes, regular monitoring of performance metrics against established benchmarks, and structured testing programs that systematically evaluate different approaches.
...
Optimization should be embedded in the execution rhythm rather than treated as an occasional review activity. Weekly performance checks identify content formats and topics generating above-average engagement for amplification. Monthly reviews assess whether platform allocation, posting frequency, and content mix are aligned with objective performance. Quarterly strategy reviews evaluate whether the fundamental strategic approach needs adjustment based on cumulative performance data, platform algorithm changes, competitive dynamics, or shifts in business objectives. Teams that build innovation in marketing into their regular optimization cycles, experimenting with new formats, platforms, and approaches on a structured basis, consistently identify growth opportunities before competitors do.

Title: Social Media Strategy Template 2026: Full Framework
URL: https://www.digitalapplied.com/blog/social-media-strategy-template-2026-full-framework
Published: 2026-04-16T23:00:00.000Z
Author: Digital Applied
Highlights:
## Key Takeaways
...
Pick two platforms, not eight:: Concentration beats coverage in 2026. Fully owning two platforms outperforms being mediocre across six, especially with creator-first algorithmic feeds.
...
Content pillars set the ceiling:: Four to six pillars with a clear ratio (e.g., 40% educate, 30% entertain, 20% inspire, 10% promote) create the system behind sustainable output.
...
Goals must fit the medium:: Social is strong on awareness, community, and retention. It is weak on direct-response pipeline when detached from paid amplification and owned channels.
...
Creators outperform brand handles:: Post-iOS 17, algorithmic feeds reward native creators. Whitelisting and paid partnerships now deliver stronger reach than pure brand-owned posting.
...
Cadence beats volume:: Posting three quality pieces a week consistently for a year outperforms daily posting that burns out after 90 days. Plan for the year, not the quarter.
...
The strategy template that worked in 2022 — pick platforms, set a cadence, post branded content, measure followers — is broken in 2026. Four structural shifts force a rewrite: platform fragmentation across eight viable networks, creator-first algorithms that discount brand handles, agentic AI search that bypasses platform discovery, and privacy changes (iOS 17 onward) that collapse classic click-through attribution. Layer on the TikTok ownership transition from 2025 and the rapid monetization of Threads and Bluesky, and the result is an operating environment where planning needs to be quarterly, not annual.
...
The shifts reward brands with clear content pillars, creator partnerships, disciplined community work, and a measurement model that treats social as part of a portfolio rather than a standalone channel. The template below is how we structure new engagements at our social media marketing practice, and how we audit incumbent strategies.
...
What social does best. KPIs: impressions, reach, video watch-time, branded search lift, share of voice. Favor platforms with strong algorithmic discovery — TikTok, Reels, YouTube Shorts. Paid amplification accelerates awareness cleanly; organic alone is slow on new handles.
...
KPIs: saves, shares, comment velocity, DM volume, community sentiment, repeat viewership. LinkedIn, Instagram, Threads, and Reddit excel here. Community is where social beats every other channel — treat DMs and comments as a product surface, not an afterthought.
...
The hardest outcome for organic social to deliver alone. KPIs: click-through to owned landing pages, form fills, demo requests, add-to-cart, assisted conversions. Use paid amplification, whitelisted creator content, and lead-gen forms on LinkedIn and Meta. Always pair social pipeline with a measured attribution model.
...
Use the matrix below to shortlist two platforms for your primary and secondary goals. The "best for" column is the realistic use case in 2026, not the platform's marketing pitch. Specs and creative templates are documented in our social media image sizes reference.
...
Rule of two: Pick one discovery platform (TikTok, Reels, Shorts, Pinterest) and one relationship platform (LinkedIn, Threads, Reddit, email). Discovery builds the audience, relationship converts it. Most single-platform strategies plateau because they skip one half of that pair.
...
Pillars are the repeatable content categories that, together, express your brand's point of view. A working pillar framework has four to six pillars, each with a fixed ratio of output, a templated format, and an owner. Without pillars, social teams burn out because every post is a new creative brief. With them, production becomes a checklist.
...
For teams running six pillars, split Educate into two (primer content for new audience + advanced content for existing audience) and add a Community pillar (reposts, UGC, member spotlights) at 10-15%. Document the pillar brief in a shared doc alongside your content calendar, not in a hundred Slack threads.
...
Cadence is where strategy meets reality. Most teams over-promise a daily rhythm they cannot sustain. The cadences below are the minimum viable and realistic maximum ranges for a brand handle in 2026. Creator channels can post more because their ratio of polish-to-output is different.
...
3-5 feed posts per week + daily Stories. Reels twice a week minimum. Fewer posts at higher quality outperforms daily mediocre output in the 2026 feed.
...
Community is the single most neglected layer of most social strategies. Teams invest 90% of effort in publishing and 10% in response — the inversion is closer to what compounds. Every comment and DM left hanging teaches the algorithm that your audience is not engaged, and teaches real humans that you do not care.
...
The 2026 benchmarks from our agency client work: DMs answered under 60 minutes during business hours, comments under 2 hours. For customer-service-heavy handles, under 30 minutes is the bar. Staff the coverage or automate initial acknowledgement with clear escalation rules.
...
In 2026, organic and paid social are one system, not two departments. Pure organic strategies plateau fast; pure paid strategies build no brand equity. The strongest framework: use organic to identify winners, use paid to amplify them, use creators to extend reach beyond your owned audience.
...
Post organically. Identify the top 10-20% of posts by save rate, share rate, and watch-time. Promote those posts with paid budget against a cold audience that mirrors your ideal customer. Retarget engagers with conversion-optimized ads toward owned landing pages. Feed the loop.
...
Whitelisted creator content — paid ads that run from the creator's handle rather than the brand's — consistently outperforms brand-produced creative by 2-4x on CTR and CPA across Meta and TikTok. Build partnership terms that include usage rights for 6-12 months and whitelisting permission up front; negotiating later is painful.
...
Typical working split for a mid-market brand: 50% paid media to cold audiences, 20% retargeting, 20% creator partnerships, 10% experimentation (new formats, new platforms, incrementality tests). Review quarterly against blended CAC and brand-lift measurement. Paired with our content operations, this ratio scales cleanly.
...
Measurement is where social strategies either earn or lose board confidence. The mistake is reporting follower count and engagement rate to executives who need to see business impact. The fix: a three-layer dashboard that maps leading indicators to business outcomes and is honest about attribution uncertainty.
...
Saves, shares, watch-time, DM volume, comment velocity, branded-search volume. These move first and predict what follower and pipeline numbers will do in 60-90 days. Make them visible to the content team so they can steer.
...
Follower growth rate (not total), reach as % of followers, engagement rate per content pillar, top-post distribution by format. Report monthly against the 90-day rolling average. Ignore vanity spikes caused by a single viral post — look at the median.
...
Branded search growth, assisted conversions from social, social-sourced pipeline, CAC contribution, customer-LTV differential for social-acquired customers. These are the numbers the C-suite cares about. Use a media-mix model or periodic incrementality tests to separate social's contribution from other channels.
...
Last-click attribution underestimates social. Data-driven attribution helps but still undervalues upper-funnel awareness. Run geo-lift or holdout tests at least annually to measure incrementality directly. Report attribution as a range, not a single number.
...
Platform-native conversion APIs are mandatory. With iOS 17 and Safari ITP, client-side pixels miss 30-60% of conversions. Implement Meta CAPI, TikTok Events API, and LinkedIn Conversions API server-side. Without them, paid optimization runs on broken signal and ad platforms cannot train their models on your actual outcomes.

To show ads that are relevant to the searches of people you're trying to reach, bundle related ads together with related keywords into an ad group. That way, all of your related ads can be shown to customers searching for similar things.
...
Although there isn’t one correct way to organize your account, this article goes over some best practices you can follow.
...
## Best practices
...
- Organize your campaigns and ad groups as you create them. Reorganizing your account after you've created several campaigns and ad groups wipes out all the valuable data you've accumulated, possibly affecting how your ads perform.
...
- When you need to use a different set of campaigns settings (ex: budget or location targeting), create a separate campaign. For instance, if you sell clothing, you may want to set a budget for shirts and a different budget for pants. In this case, you should create a campaign for shirts, and another campaign for pants.
...
- If you want all your ads to share a budget and you want to target the same set of locations, then you can just use one campaign.
...
- For each ad group, pick a narrow theme and create ad groups around that theme. For each ad group, use keywords related to that theme. Consider also having your ads mention at least one of your keywords in its headline. This is because when someone searches for a term that matches your keyword, and they see one ad that mentions the keyword and another ad that doesn't mention the keyword, they are more likely to think that the first ad is more relevant to what they're searching for.
...
- You never know what message might resonate best with potential customers. Because of this, we recommend having at least 3 ads in each ad group and using optimized ad rotation. The optimize setting will optimize your ads for clicks in each individual auction using signals like keyword, search term, device, location, and more. Learn about Ad rotation settings.
...
## Organize your campaigns
...
A campaign consists of a collection of ad groups. For your campaign, consider the following:
...
- How much you're willing to spend on clicks or conversions from your ads
- Networks and geographical locations where you want your ads to show.
- Other top-level settings that affect clusters of ad groups
...
For example, let’s say you want to create ads for your inventory of televisions and cameras, so you create separate campaigns for each.
...
## Organize your ad groups
...
A common method for organizing a Google Ads account is to organize with a category or business goal in mind. For many businesses, the way your website is structured is a good place to start, where each ad group representing a different page or category on your site.
...
Ad groups can help ensure that your ads are relevant to your keywords. For example, for your camera campaign, you may want to create separate ads for digital cameras and compact cameras. You can create ad groups for each type of camera, each with its own set of focused, relevant keywords. For your television campaign, you can also create an ad group for each type of television you sell, such as flat screen and plasma TVs.
...
Within a particular ad group, you'll want to choose keywords that are closely linked to your ad text. For your digital cameras ad group, you might try keywords for different brands, models, and prices for the digital cameras you sell.
...
Tying it all together, your overall account structure might look like this:

Title: Content Marketing Strategy: How to Build One in 2026
URL: https://marketful.com/content-marketing-strategy
Published: 2026-06-17T11:35:56.000Z
Author: N/A
Highlights:
Build a content marketing strategy that drives pipeline in 2026. Covers the 6-part framework, B2B buying committee matrix, AEO distribution layer, and trust measurement.
...
A content marketing strategy is a documented plan that defines what content you'll create, for whom, through which channels, and how you'll measure success. It connects every piece of content to a business goal.
...
This guide covers what a complete content marketing strategy includes, how to build one in nine steps, what 2026 changes about distribution, and the measurement gap most programs haven't closed.
...
- Only 47% of B2B marketers have a documented content strategy (CMI, 2025).
- A complete strategy has six interdependent components: goals, audience, content architecture, production, distribution, and measurement.
- AI answer engines (ChatGPT, Perplexity, Gemini) are now a B2B buying touchpoint, not a trend to watch.
- Standard analytics confirm content was consumed. Almost none confirm whether buyers trust you.
- Only 3% of your target market is actively buying at any moment. Most strategy time should go toward the 97%.
...
## The 6-Component Content Marketing Strategy Framework
...
After synthesizing Block Club's B2B framework, CMI's measurement model, HubSpot's content playbook, and DemandNexus's buying-committee approach, the consensus structure has six interdependent parts.
...
### 1. Strategic Foundation (Goals and Objectives)
...
Define what content must do for the business before choosing formats or topics. Common goal categories for B2B: brand awareness and thought leadership, demand generation and lead capture, pipeline acceleration, customer retention and expansion.
...
### 2. Audience Intelligence
...
For B2B, you need more than one buyer persona. Map the full buying committee. A VP of Marketing and a CTO have fundamentally different information needs.
...
### 3. Content Architecture (Pillars and Topic Clusters)
...
Define 3-5 content pillars: core themes the brand will consistently own. Pillars should reflect buyer pain areas, not product categories.
...
### 4. Content Production and Editorial Process
...
An editorial calendar defines themes across time. A content calendar details individual pieces with keyword, persona, funnel stage, owner, and publish date. The workflow runs: ideation, brief, writing, review, approval, optimization, publishing.
...
### 5. Distribution and Promotion Plan
...
Content creation is half the work. Most programs underinvest in the other half. Block Club recommends putting 25-35% of the content budget toward distribution for mature B2B programs.
...
Channel stack for B2B, ranked by ICP precision: owned media (website, email, LinkedIn) first, then niche industry media, webinar co-hosting, content syndication, LinkedIn paid, community seeding, and partnerships. In 2026, add AEO as a mandatory layer. More on this in Step 8.
...
### 6. Measurement and Analytics
...
KPIs come before copy. Three standard categories: reach and awareness, engagement and pipeline, revenue impact. The fourth category most programs skip: trust measurement.
...
## How to Build a Content Marketing Strategy in 9 Steps
...
### Step 1: Define Goals
...
### Step 2: Research Your Audience
...
Analyze CRM data, sales call recordings, and social analytics. For B2B, map the full buying committee. Refresh personas annually.
...
### Step 3: Run a Content Audit
...
If you have existing content: inventory it, tag each piece (keep, update, consolidate, remove), and identify topic gaps, freshness issues, and repurposing opportunities. See content refresh for the step-by-step process. New programs skip to step 4.
...
### Step 4: Choose Your CMS and Governance Model
...
Pick a CMS your team will actually use. Set governance standards: who can publish, what review process applies, and what style guidelines govern output. Without governance, content debt accumulates faster than you can produce.
...
### Step 5: Determine Content Types and Formats
...
### Step 6: Map Content to the Buyer Journey
...
At any given moment, only 3% of your target market is actively in a buying phase, as TK Kader documents in detail below. The other 97% need content that helps them identify problems and understand solutions before they're ready to compare vendors.
...
### Step 7: Build the Distribution Engine
...
Plan distribution before writing. Answer "where will this live after publication?" before you write the first sentence. Practitioners who do this make better format decisions and reach higher actual audiences.
...
### Step 8: Add the AEO Layer
...
45% of buyers now use AI for initial research and 67% prefer a rep-free buying experience (Robert Rose, CMI, May 2026). AEO structural tactics: lead each page with a direct declarative answer, build entity density (name specific tools, frameworks, companies), make H2 headings answer specific questions, treat FAQ sections as strategic citation assets.
...
### Step 9: Implement Measurement From Day One
...
CMI's 5-stage measurement framework starts with Stage 1 See Us (reach, traffic, AI visibility) and Stage 2 Connect (engagement, time on page, email CTR). Stages 3-5 track Trust Us (returning visitor rate, webinar attendance), Choose Us (lead forms, content-assisted conversions), and Champion Us (retention rates, referrals). Reporting solely on Stage 1 makes the content program look like a PR campaign and vulnerable to budget cuts.
...
Add trust measurement via human input: customer councils, advisory dinners, and expert calls capture what analytics cannot. Standard content marketing metrics tell you what happened. Human conversations tell you what it meant to buyers.
...
## Content by Funnel Stage
...
, newsletters.
...
### Bottom of Funnel (Decision)
...
Goal: remove objections and provide social proof. Formats: customer success stories with quantified outcomes, security and compliance documentation for enterprise buyers, implementation roadmaps, competitive displacement content, and pricing pages (repeated visits are the highest-intent signal in your CMS analytics).
...
## The Measurement Layer Most Teams Skip
...
The attribution method: track content consumption events per contact in your CRM, flag opportunities where a known contact consumed content before the opportunity was created, then sum the deal value of "content-touched" opportunities.
...
Practitioners who add human-input measurement (customer advisory boards, post-purchase interviews, win/loss analysis tied to content touchpoints) surface what analytics miss. It's the difference between knowing content was consumed and knowing it contributed to a buying decision.
...
## B2B vs B2C: The Core Differences
...
## Content Repurposing as Extraction
...
The standard repurposing approach: take a long-form article and reformat it for other channels. The more productive framing: treat the original source as raw material to mine, not a piece to resize.
...
The 4-bucket extraction method applied to any long-form source:
...
A 45-minute recorded expert conversation mined this way generates short-form video clips, LinkedIn posts, email newsletter snippets, FAQ sections, and a blog post, all from the original source material. Repurposing at this level delivers 3–5x distribution reach at 20–30% of equivalent net-new production cost. Most programs underperform because they reformat instead of extract.
...
## Budget Allocation for Content Marketing
...
## Common Content Marketing Strategy Mistakes
...
### Treating traffic as the primary goal
...
Traffic without pipeline contribution is a vanity metric. Define what you're optimizing for: MQLs, pipeline volume, or deal velocity. Then work backward to the content that moves those numbers.
...
### Publishing without a distribution plan

### Building content for one persona in a committee buying process

### Ignoring AI search as a distribution channel

### Scaling production before proving quality

Title: Content Strategy: What Is It & How to Create One (2025)
URL: https://neilpatel.com/blog/content-strategy-a-development-guide/
Published: 2024-12-24T20:00:00.000Z
Author: Neil Patel
Highlights:
# Content Strategy: What Is It & How to Create One (2025)
...
## Key Takeaways
...
- Developing a comprehensive content creation strategy ensures marketing departments use resources efficiently and effectively to plan and optimize their efforts. The different components include defining your goals and understanding your audience.
- Defining the goals for a content strategy can indicate the types of content businesses should focus on.
...
- Researching audiences to understand the content topics and formats they are most likely to engage with is critical in a content creation strategy.
- In addition to creating content, marketers need to focus on how to distribute it and how to measure its effectiveness, including the revenue it brings in.
...
But here’s the thing: Successful content creation doesn’t happen by chance. It requires a well-thought-out content creation strategy that aligns with your goals, engages your audience, focuses on quality content, and measures its impact.
...
## What Is a Content Strategy?
...
A content creation strategy is a detailed plan for creating, distributing, managing, and measuring the effect of marketing content designed to appeal to prospective customers across all stages of the marketing funnel.
...
Key elements of a successful content marketing strategy framework are:
...
- Defining the goals and objectives.
- Deciding on content types.
- Conducting audience and keyword research with content marketing tools.
- Selecting the platforms where you’ll share your content.
...
## Step 1: Define Your Goals for Your Content Strategy
...
Defining your goals is vital when developing a content creation strategy.
...
## Step #2: Research Your Audience for Your Content Strategy
...
Before you create a content strategy, consider this: It will only be effective once you know who your target audience is. You’ll need to research their age, location, interests, the kind of content they already engage with, and which social media platforms they are on. These are all important details you can use in your content planning.
...
## Step 3: Analyze Your Competition
...
Identify four or five of your biggest competitors and analyze their content. Explore their topics, formats (such as blog posts, videos, or social media posts), and the frequency with which they publish content.
...
Investigate which topics and formats get the most engagement in terms of comments and shares, and analyze which topics they are (or aren’t) covering. Read user comments to understand the appeal.
...
Use tools like Ubersuggest to analyze their keyword and content strategies. You can use your findings to target similar keywords and topics. You can also use it to research what content topics your competitors have written about.
...
## Step 4: Run a Content Audit
...
A content audit helps you identify gaps, refresh outdated content, and repurpose existing assets. For example, an old blog post might be updated with new statistics, or a webinar recording can be repackaged into a blog post.
...
- Content performance: Which pieces are gaining traffic, engagement, and conversions, and which need updating or removal?
- New SEO opportunities: Refresh content with new keyword targets and fill keyword gaps.
- Assessing content: Analyzing for relevance, accuracy, and freshness and deciding if content needs refreshing.
- Content duplication: Finding content you can merge or delete to avoid content or keyword cannibalization.
- Search intent: Determining if the content meets the user’s search intent and realigning content if needed.
...
To conduct an audit, create a spreadsheet to inventory every piece of content by type. This can include web pages, blog posts, social media posts, emails and newsletters, and documents and media. Organize them by format.
...
## Step 5: Brainstorm Content Topics and Formats
...
Based on what you’ve learned from completing the above steps, you should have enough information to develop a well-crafted content creation strategy that aligns with your business goals, resonates with your audience, and integrates with your SEO strategy and keyword targets.
...
To achieve this, use the methods I’ve described so far. For example, use your customer persona to attract your ideal customers, Then, fill content gaps using your audit and competitor analysis. Next, integrate this with your SEO strategy to develop keyword targets based on your findings from the steps above.
...
## Step 6: Build a Content Calendar and Publish
...
Developing a content strategy is one thing, but keeping everything on track can be a struggle. That’s why you’ll want a content calendar. A calendar gives you a space to plan, brainstorm, and schedule content, improving consistency. It also helps you stay aligned with your goals, increasing your chances of success.
...
## Step 7: Amplify Your Content Strategy
...
A sound content marketing strategy framework and targeted content creation are only part of the equation. It’s incomplete unless you get that content in front of your audience.
...
You could:
...
## Step 8: Measure Your Results for Your Content Strategy
...
Successful content marketing means knowing what your audience liked and what they didn’t. You can only understand that if you measure your content marketing strategy results. By measuring key metrics and seeing what’s working, you have cold, hard data to apply to your future content and get better results.
...
### 1. Consumption Metrics
...
### 2. Social Sharing Metrics
...
Sharing on platforms like social media can be a great metric for judging your audience’s engagement. Ask yourself what type of content gets the most shares, who is sharing, which platforms are involved, and, most importantly, what content is converting. Google Analytics helps you better understand user behavior and measure social media ROI.
...
### 3. Lead Metrics
...
### 4. Sales Metrics
...
For most businesses, content generation should ultimately boost revenue. Therefore, if you have a revenue-based content marketing strategy, you need to measure the revenue generated by your content marketing.
...
## Step 9: Listen to Your Customers to Improve Your Content Strategy
...
Different phases of the customer journey require different content topics and formats. You’ll need to figure out what types of content will attract users at each stage. In other words, you have to develop the right message at the right time for the right people as part of your content creation strategy.
...
### What is involved in content strategy?
...
Creating a content strategy requires considering your goals, researching your audience, analyzing your competitors’ content, and auditing your existing content. It also involves deciding how you will distribute, promote, and measure the effectiveness of your content.
...
The first step to creating a content strategy should be to define your goals. This will help guide who your target audience is, what kind of content you want to create, and where you will distribute it.
...
### What is a content strategy framework?
...
A content strategy framework is an outline of how you plan to create content. A framework might include your audience, keywords you will target, how often and when you publish content, and who will create the content.
...
### Why is content strategy important?
...
If you want to rank high in search results, you need quality content. You also need good content, so when people visit your website, they want to convert. Content that doesn’t reflect your audience’s needs or interests won’t be effective.
...
Following the steps outlined here will help you create an effective content strategy and identify key metrics to help you monitor the performance of your content. Still, expect to go through some trial and error when you start. Once you have a recipe for success, though, all you’ll have to do is rinse and repeat to see the kind of audience loyalty and growth you’ve always dreamed of!

input.content:
# Marketing Documentation

A curated set of marketing reference docs compiled from authoritative sources on
the web. Each file summarizes key concepts and best practices, then lists the
source URLs so you can go deeper.

> Note: Snippets were gathered via web search. Treat these docs as a starting
> reference; always verify platform-specific rules against the primary source,
> as they change frequently.

## Contents

| File | Topic |
| --- | --- |
| [01-digital-marketing-fundamentals.md](01-digital-marketing-fundamentals.md) | Core channels, media models, funnel, measurement |
| [02-seo.md](02-seo.md) | Search engine optimization (Google-first) |
| [03-email-marketing.md](03-email-marketing.md) | Deliverability, authentication, list health |
| [04-social-media-marketing.md](04-social-media-marketing.md) | Platform strategy, content, engagement |
| [05-paid-ads-ppc.md](05-paid-ads-ppc.md) | Google Ads structure & campaign best practices |
| [06-content-marketing.md](06-content-marketing.md) | Strategy framework, production, distribution |

## How the channels fit together

The dominant theme across every source: **integration**. No single channel wins
alone. A strong system looks like this:

- **Owned media** (site, blog, email list) — assets you control and that compound.
- **Earned media** (press, shares, reviews, word of mouth) — highest credibility.
- **Paid media** (search/social/display ads) — fast, controllable, stops when budget stops.

SEO feeds content, content fuels social and email, and paid amplifies the best of it.

## The customer funnel (applied digitally)

1. **Awareness** — SEO, social, paid ads.
2. **Consideration** — content, email, reviews.
3. **Conversion** — landing pages, offers, checkout.
4. **Retention & loyalty** — email, community, post-purchase experience.

_Last compiled: 2026-07-10_

state.title: marketing-docs/README.md

input.content:
# Digital Marketing Fundamentals

## Definition

Digital marketing is the promotion of products, services, or brands through
internet-connected channels and technologies — search engines, social media,
email, websites, and mobile apps. The American Marketing Association defines it
broadly as "any marketing methods conducted through electronic devices which
utilize some form of a computer."

The defining difference from traditional marketing is **measurability**: nearly
every click, open, and purchase is trackable, turning marketing from guesswork
into a discipline.

## The durable fundamentals (they outlast tactics)

1. **Core channels** — SEO, content, social, email, paid ads, affiliate/influencer.
2. **The owned / earned / paid media model.**
3. **A data & measurement loop.**
4. **The customer funnel applied across digital touchpoints.**

Tools and platforms change; these foundations don't.

## The core channels

| Channel | What it does | Character |
| --- | --- | --- |
| SEO | Earn organic visibility when people search | Durable, high-trust, compounds (3–6+ mo) |
| Content marketing | Attract by being useful, not interruptive | Compounds; fuels other channels |
| Social media | Build audience, community, brand | Discovery + relationship |
| Email | Nurture an owned audience directly | Highest ROI (~$36–42 per $1) |
| Paid ads / PPC | Buy attention & traffic | Fast, controllable; stops with budget |
| Affiliate / influencer | Reach via partners' borrowed trust | Leverage others' audiences |

## Owned, Earned, Paid media

- **Owned** — your website, blog, email list, app. You control them; they compound.
- **Earned** — press, shares, reviews, word of mouth. Can't buy directly; most credible.
- **Paid** — ads across search/social/display. Fast and controllable but rented.

A strong strategy balances all three: paid rents demand, owned builds assets,
earned grants credibility.

## The data & measurement loop

- Visibility into what works lets you find and fix drop-off points.
- Modern principle: **"measure what matters, respect the customer, act on the
  data you can ethically collect"** — not "track everything" (privacy changes,
  cookie restrictions, regulation).
- Metrics that matter: CAC (customer acquisition cost), ROAS (return on ad
  spend), CPL (cost per lead), CLV/LTV (lifetime value), conversion rate.
- ROI formula: `(Revenue - Marketing Cost) / Marketing Cost × 100`.
- Rule of thumb benchmarks cited: aim for ~3:1 ROAS on paid, ~5:1 on organic.

## A starter framework

| Step | Focus | Why |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | Fundamentals | Understand channels, media model, data, funnel |
| 2 | One channel | Build real competence in a single discipline |
| 3 | A real project | Apply with actual stakes and feedback |
| 4 | Analytics | Set up measurement to learn what works |
| 5 | Integration | Add channels and connect them into one system |

## 8-step strategy framework

1. Define business objectives & SMART marketing goals.
2. Understand your audience deeply.
3. Audit your current digital presence.
4. Select your channel mix.
5. Develop your content strategy.
6. Set budget and allocate resources.
7. Implement tracking & attribution.
8. Launch, measure, and optimize.

## What's changing in 2026 (AI era)

- Discovery is going **visual and conversational**: Google AI Overviews (~16% of
  queries), ChatGPT search, Perplexity, Gemini, and platform-native search
  (TikTok, YouTube, Reddit).
- New optimization layers: **AEO** (Answer Engine Optimization — get cited by AI
  engines) and **GEO** (Generative Engine Optimization — content shaped for AI
  synthesis).
- Privacy-first marketing and first-party data ownership are now essential.
- The fundamentals stay the same; execution becomes faster, more personalized,
  more visual.

## Typical result timelines

- Paid (PPC/social ads): leads within 24–48 hours.
- Social organic: engagement in days, conversion tuning 2–4 weeks.
- SEO: meaningful movement in 3–6 months, big gains 6–12 months.
- Email (existing list): immediate.

## Sources

- What is digital marketing? — Adobe: https://business.adobe.com/blog/basics/digital-marketing
- Digital Marketing Complete Guide (2026) — PxlPeak: https://pxlpeak.com/guide/digital-marketing-complete-guide
- Fundamentals of Digital Marketing (2026) — BrandClickX: https://brandclickx.com/fundamentals-of-digital-marketing/
- Digital Marketing: The Complete 2026 Guide — Frostbite: https://frostbitemarketing.com/resources/guides/digital-marketing-guide-2026/
- What Is Digital Marketing? A Complete 2026 Guide — Three Girls Media: https://threegirlsmedia.co.uk/digital-marketing-explained-a-complete-2026-guide/
- Ultimate Guide to Digital Marketing (PDF) — DigitalMarketer: https://www.digitalmarketer.com/digital-marketing/assets/pdf/ultimate-guide-to-digital-marketing.pdf

+
+## A starter framework
+
+| Step | Focus | Why |
+| --- | --- | --- |
+| 1 | Fundamentals | Understand channels, media model, data, funnel |
+| 2 | One channel | Build real competence in a single discipline |
+| 3 | A real project | Apply with actual stakes and feedback |
+| 4 | Analytics | Set up measurement to learn what works |
+| 5 | Integration | Add channels and connect them into one system |
+
+## 8-step strategy framework
+
+1. Define business objectives & SMART marketing goals.
+2. Understand your audience deeply.
+3. Audit your current digital presence.
+4. Select your channel mix.
+5. Develop your content strategy.
+6. Set budget and allocate resources.
+7. Implement tracking & attribution.
+8. Launch, measure, and optimize.
+
+## What's changing in 2026 (AI era)
+
+- Discovery is going **visual and conversational**: Google AI Overviews (~16% of
+  queries), ChatGPT search, Perplexity, Gemini, and platform-native search
+  (TikTok, YouTube, Reddit).
+- New optimization layers: **AEO** (Answer Engine Optimization — get cited by AI
+  engines) and **GEO** (Generative Engine Optimization — content shaped for AI
+  synthesis).
+- Privacy-first marketing and first-party data ownership are now essential.
+- The fundamentals stay the same; execution becomes faster, more personalized,
+  more visual.
+
+## Typical result timelines
+
+- Paid (PPC/social ads): leads within 24–48 hours.
+- Social organic: engagement in days, conversion tuning 2–4 weeks.
+- SEO: meaningful movement in 3–6 months, big gains 6–12 months.
+- Email (existing list): immediate.
+
+## Sources
+
+- What is digital marketing? — Adobe: https://business.adobe.com/blog/basics/digital-marketing
+- Digital Marketing Complete Guide (2026) — PxlPeak: https://pxlpeak.com/guide/digital-marketing-complete-guide
+- Fundamentals of Digital Marketing (2026) — BrandClickX: https://brandclickx.com/fundamentals-of-digital-marketing/
+- Digital Marketing: The Complete 2026 Guide — Frostbite: https://frostbitemarketing.com/resources/guides/digital-marketing-guide-2026/

+- Ultimate Guide to Digital Marketing (PDF) — DigitalMarketer: https://www.digitalmarketer.com/digital-marketing/assets/pdf/ultimate-guide-to-digital-marketing.pdf

+++ /home/uzer/marketing-docs/01-digital-marketing-fundamentals.md
@@ -0,0 +1,102 @@
+# Digital Marketing Fundamentals
+
+## Definition
+
+Digital marketing is the promotion of products, services, or brands through
+internet-connected channels and technologies — search engines, social media,
+email, websites, and mobile apps. The American Marketing Association defines it
+broadly as "any marketing methods conducted through electronic devices which
+utilize some form of a computer."
+
+The defining difference from traditional marketing is **measurability**: nearly
+every click, open, and purchase is trackable, turning marketing from guesswork
+into a discipline.
+
+## The durable fundamentals (they outlast tactics)
+
+1. **Core channels** — SEO, content, social, email, paid ads, affiliate/influencer.
+2. **The owned / earned / paid media model.**
+3. **A data & measurement loop.**
+4. **The customer funnel applied across digital touchpoints.**
+
+Tools and platforms change; these foundations don't.
+
+## The core channels
+
+| Channel | What it does | Character |
+| --- | --- | --- |
+| SEO | Earn organic visibility when people search | Durable, high-trust, compounds (3–6+ mo) |
+| Content marketing | Attract by being useful, not interruptive | Compounds; fuels other channels |
+| Social media | Build audience, community, brand | Discovery + relationship |
+| Email | Nurture an owned audience directly | Highest ROI (~$36–42 per $1) |
+| Paid ads / PPC | Buy attention & traffic | Fast, controllable; stops with budget |
+| Affiliate / influencer | Reach via partners' borrowed trust | Leverage others' audiences |
+
+## Owned, Earned, Paid media
+
+- **Owned** — your website, blog, email list, app. You control them; they compound.
+- **Earned** — press, shares, reviews, word of mouth. Can't buy directly; most credible.
+- **Paid** — ads across search/social/display. Fast and controllable but rented.
+
+A strong strategy balances all three: paid rents demand, owned builds assets,
+earned grants credibility.
+
+## The data & measurement loop
+
+- Visibility into what works lets you find and fix drop-off points.
+- Modern principle: **"measure what matters, respect the customer, act on the
+  data you can ethically collect"** — not "track everything" (privacy changes,
+  cookie restrictions, regulation).
+- Metrics that matter: CAC (customer acquisition cost), ROAS (return on ad
+  spend), CPL (cost per lead), CLV/LTV (lifetime value), conversion rate.
+- ROI formula: `(Revenue - Marketing Cost) / Marketing Cost × 100`.
+- Rule of thumb benchmarks cited: aim for ~3:1 ROAS on paid, ~5:1 on organic.
+
+## A starter framework
+
+| Step | Focus | Why |
+| --- | --- | --- |
+| 1 | Fundamentals | Understand channels, media model, data, funnel |
+| 2 | One channel | Build real competence in a single discipline |
+| 3 | A real project | Apply with actual stakes and feedback |
+| 4 | Analytics | Set up measurement to learn what works |
+| 5 | Integration | Add channels and connect them into one system |
+
+## 8-step strategy framework
+
+1. Define business objectives & SMART marketing goals.
+2. Understand your audience deeply.
+3. Audit your current digital presence.
+4. Select your channel mix.
+5. Develop your content strategy.
+6. Set budget and allocate resources.
+7. Implement tracking & attribution.
+8. Launch, measure, and optimize.
+
+## What's changing in 2026 (AI era)
+
+- Discovery is going **visual and conversational**: Google AI Overviews (~16% of
+  queries), ChatGPT search, Perplexity, Gemini, and platform-native search
+  (TikTok, YouTube, Reddit).
+- New optimization layers: **AEO** (Answer Engine Optimization — get cited by AI
+  engines) and **GEO** (Generative Engine Optimization — content shaped for AI
+  synthesis).
+- Privacy-first marketing and first-party data ownership are now essential.
+- The fundamentals stay the same; execution becomes faster, more personalized,
+  more visual.
+
+## Typical result timelines
+
+- Paid (PPC/social ads): leads within 24–48 hours.
+- Social organic: engagement in days, conversion tuning 2–4 weeks.
+- SEO: meaningful movement in 3–6 months, big gains 6–12 months.
+- Email (existing list): immediate.
+
+## Sources
+
+- What is digital marketing? — Adobe: https://business.adobe.com/blog/basics/digital-marketing

diff --git a/marketing-docs/02-seo.md b/marketing-docs/02-seo.md
new file mode 100644
index 00000000..d6f3a14c
--- /dev/null
+++ b/marketing-docs/02-seo.md
@@ -0,0 +1,91 @@
+# SEO (Search Engine Optimization)
+
+Google-first reference, based on Google Search Central documentation plus current
+2026 guidance (AEO/GEO for AI search).
+
+## What SEO is
+
+The practice of improving your website's content, structure, and authority so it
+ranks higher in **unpaid (organic) search results**. One of the highest-ROI
+channels because traffic is "free" once rankings are achieved, and it compounds.
+
+## How Google Search works (crawl → index → serve)
+
+- **Googlebot** discovers URLs by crawling links, sitemaps, and redirects.
+- Pages are **indexed** (added to the index) if reachable and not blocked.
+- Google **serves** relevant results to queries.
+
+Key implication: if Googlebot can't find or understand a page, it can't rank.
+
+## Essentials (Google Search Essentials)
+
+- Create **helpful, reliable, people-first content**.
+- Use words people actually search for, and place them naturally on the page.
+- Make every page crawlable and indexable.
+- Avoid deceptive or manipulative practices (cloaking, hidden text, link schemes).
+
+## Beginner checklist (SEO Starter Guide)
+
+1. **Descriptive page titles & meta descriptions** — unique per page; help users
+   see relevance in results and can lift click-through.
+2. **Use words people search for** in headings and body copy.
+3. **Clear, logical site structure** — link important URLs together with
+   descriptive anchor text.
+4. **Images**: use descriptive filenames and `alt` text.
+5. **Links**: build genuine, relevant links; use `rel="nofollow"` for paid/
+   untrusted/required-login links.
+6. **Duplicate content**: consolidate with canonical tags; pick one canonical URL.
+7. **Mobile-friendly & fast**: Google uses a mobile crawler by default. Core Web
+   Vitals matter.
+8. **Secure (HTTPS)**: preferred and a ranking/trust signal.
+9. **Videos**: provide text context; Googlebot can't read text inside video.
+
+## Developer / technical SEO
+
+- Ensure all pages are reachable by an **anchor link** from another findable page.
+- Submit an XML **sitemap** to encourage/smaritize crawling of key pages.
+- For JS apps with one HTML page, give **each screen/content piece its own URL**.
+- Keep key info in **text, not graphics**; use **semantic HTML**.
+- Make content available in the **DOM** (CSS `content` property is not indexed).
+- **robots.txt** blocks crawling; `noindex` blocks indexing but allows crawling.
+- Use **canonical** tags to manage duplicate URLs.
+- Use **301** redirects for permanent moves, **302** for temporary.
+- Add **structured data** (schema) to enable rich results.
+- Use URL Inspection / Rich Results Test to see how Google views your site.
+- Manage crawl budget for very large sites via sitemaps + robots.txt.
+
+## 2026 evolution: AEO & GEO
+
+- **AEO (Answer Engine Optimization)** — get cited by ChatGPT, Perplexity,
+  Gemini. Tactics: structured data, topic authority, `llms.txt` directives.
+- **GEO (Generative Engine Optimization)** — shape content for AI synthesis:
+  clear topic sentences, entity-rich phrasing, strong source-quality signals.
+- ~45% of B2B buyers now use AI for initial research. Treat AI engines as a
+  distribution channel, not a trend.
+
+## Local SEO
+
+- **Google Business Profile** wins the map pack; critical for service-area
+  businesses.
+- Build citations + reviews; keep NAP (name/address/phone) consistent.
+
+## Timelines
+
+- Traditional SEO: meaningful results in 3–6 months, compounding gains 6–18 months.
+- Google Business Profile map-pack movement: ~3–8 weeks.
+- AEO/structured-data citation: ~4–10 weeks.
+
+## Measurement
+
+- Google Search Console (indexing, queries, clicks, Core Web Vitals).
+- Google Analytics 4 (engagement, conversions).
+- Track rankings, impressions, CTR, organic conversions, and now AI visibility.
+
+## Sources
+
+- SEO Starter Guide — Google Search Central: https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/seo-starter-guide
+- Google Search Essentials (guidelines): https://developers.google.com/search/docs/essentials
+- SEO documentation hub: https://developers.google.com/search/docs
+- SEO Guide for Web Developers: https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/get-started-developers
+- Technical SEO techniques: https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/get-started
+- SEO Starter Guide makeover (what changed): https://developers.google.com/search/blog/2024/02/ssg-gets-a-makeover

diff --git a/marketing-docs/03-email-marketing.md b/marketing-docs/03-email-marketing.md
new file mode 100644
index 00000000..e2afb2ee
--- /dev/null
+++ b/marketing-docs/03-email-marketing.md
@@ -0,0 +1,92 @@
+# Email Marketing
+
+Focus: deliverability and compliance (getting to the inbox), list health, and
+best practices. Email remains one of the highest-ROI channels (~$36–42 per $1
+spent).
+
+## Why deliverability matters
+
+Great content is worthless if it lands in spam. Inbox placement depends on
+**sender reputation**, authentication, list quality, sending behavior, and
+engagement signals.
+
+## Authentication (mandatory)
+
+Set up all three DNS-based standards:
+
+- **SPF (Sender Policy Framework)** — authorizes which servers may send for your
+  domain. Keep the record updated; don't exceed **10 DNS lookups** (SPF auto-fails).
+- **DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail)** — cryptographic signature proving the
+  domain owner sent the message. Use 1024- or 2048-bit keys.
+- **DMARC (Domain-Based Message Authentication, Reporting & Conformance)** —
+  tells receivers what to do on auth failure. Start at `p=none` (monitor), then
+  move to `p=quarantine` or `p=reject`. Requires **domain alignment**: the `From`
+  domain must match the one passing SPF or DKIM.
+- **BIMI (Brand Indicators for Message Identification)** — shows your logo in
+  inbox; requires DMARC `p=quarantine`/`reject` + a VMC (Verified Mark Certificate).
+
+Gmail requirement (Feb 1, 2024): senders of **>5,000 messages/day** to Gmail must
+have SPF + DKIM + DMARC, valid forward/reverse DNS (PTR), TLS, and **one-click
+unsubscribe**.
+
+## Sender reputation & monitoring
+
+- Use **dedicated vs shared IP** intentionally; shared IPs tie your fate to others.
+- Monitor with Google Postmaster Tools, Sender Score, and your ESP dashboards.
+- Keep **spam rate below 0.10%**, never reaching 0.30% (Gmail/Yahoo guidance).
+- A single spam-trap hit can take 6–12 months to recover from.
+
+## List health (permission-first)
+
+- Use **opt-in**; double opt-in is best practice to block invalid signups.
+- **Never buy lists** — they're full of unengaged contacts and tank deliverability.
+- Prune invalid/bounced/unsubscribed addresses; verify emails.
+- Segment by interest/behavior for relevance.
+- Honor unsubscribes within **48 hours**; one-click unsubscribe is required for
+  bulk senders.
+- Suppress spam complainers automatically.
+
+## Engagement benchmarks (Klaviyo reference)
+
+- Open rate ≥ 33% (note: opens are less reliable post Apple MPP).
+- Click rate ≥ 1%.
+- Bounce rate < 1.0%.
+- Unsubscribe rate < 0.3%.
+
+Engagement (opens, replies, clicks) directly influences inbox placement.
+
+## Content & formatting
+
+- **Subject lines**: honest, no ALL CAPS, no spammy words/excessive punctuation,
+  avoid clickbait. Use 3–5 words + strong preview text.
+- **HTML**: clean, well-formed; include a **plain-text version**; balance
+  text-to-image ratio (don't send one giant image). Keep email < ~100KB.
+- **Images**: add alt text; host on trusted domains/CDNs.
+- **Links**: use your own domain or ESP-tracked HTTPS links; avoid URL
+  shorteners (flagged by spam filters); check links for blacklisting/malware.
+- Include physical mailing address in footer + visible unsubscribe (CAN-SPAM,
+  GDPR, etc.).
+
+## Sending practices
+
+- **Warm up** new/cold IPs and new domains (esp. <30 days old) gradually.
+- Start low (e.g., 1–2K/day per provider), ramp slowly as engagement stays solid.
+- Send at a **consistent cadence**; avoid big bursts after silence.
+- Monitor SMTP responses (e.g., `4.7.28` / `421` = slow down).
+- Target engaged users first to build reputation.
+
+## Compliance
+
+- **CAN-SPAM (US)**: no deceptive headers/subjects, opt-out honored, physical
+  address, no pre-checked boxes.
+- **GDPR (EU)**: lawful basis + easy withdrawal; honor regional rules.
+- **Gmail/Yahoo bulk sender rules** (2024): apply to any sender >5k/day.
+
+## Sources
+
+- Gmail Email sender guidelines: https://support.google.com/mail/answer/81126
+- Email Deliverability Checklist (2026) — WordStream: https://www.wordstream.com/blog/email-deliverability-checklist
+- Email deliverability best practices — Klaviyo: https://help.klaviyo.com/hc/en-us/articles/25620771311643
+- Email deliverability best practice guide — Adobe: https://business.adobe.com/resources/sdk/adobe-experience-platform-email-deliverability-best-practice-guide.html
+- 2025 Marketer's Guide to Email Deliverability — Litmus: https://www.litmus.com/blog/why-email-deliverability-matters
+- What is Email Deliverability? — Salesforce: https://www.salesforce.com/marketing/email/deliverability/

diff --git a/marketing-docs/04-social-media-marketing.md b/marketing-docs/04-social-media-marketing.md
new file mode 100644
index 00000000..9d128241
--- /dev/null
+++ b/marketing-docs/04-social-media-marketing.md
@@ -0,0 +1,88 @@
+# Social Media Marketing
+
+SMM = using social platforms to connect with your audience to build brand,
+increase sales, and drive traffic — via publishing, engagement, listening, and
+paid social ads.
+
+## The #1 principle: focus, don't spray
+
+- **Pick 1–2 primary platforms** where your audience actually spends time. A
+  dominant presence on 2 beats a mediocre presence on 5–6.
+- Random acts of content lead to random results. Strategy first.
+- Use the **"rule of two"**: one **discovery** platform (TikTok, Reels, Shorts,
+  Pinterest) + one **relationship** platform (LinkedIn, Threads, Reddit, email).
+
+## The 7-step strategy cycle (Buffer)
+
+1. Set **SMART goals**.
+2. Define your **target audience**.
+3. Choose **content pillars** (repeatable themes).
+4. Choose your **platforms** (where audience is).
+5. Build a **content calendar**.
+6. **Analyze** performance.
+7. **Evaluate** and iterate (it's a flywheel, not a one-time plan).
+
+## Content pillars & ratio
+
+- Most successful brands use a **70/30 value-to-promo** split (or 60/25/15:
+  60% educational, 25% personal/brand, 15% promotional).
+- A working pillar set = 4–6 pillars, each with a fixed output ratio, format, and
+  owner. Example: 40% educate, 30% entertain, 20% inspire, 10% promote.
+- **UGC (user-generated content)** can see ~8.7x higher engagement than
+  brand-produced content in some verticals — audiences trust peers.
+
+## Platform notes (2026 benchmarks)
+
+- **TikTok** — best organic reach (~5.3% engagement). Vertical 9:16, hook in
+  first 2 sec, trending audio, authenticity over polish. Strong for under-35 B2C.
+- **LinkedIn** — ~6.5% organic engagement; **personal posts get ~561% more reach
+  than company pages**. Best for B2B. Long-form text, document carousels, native
+  video, thoughtful comments.
+- **Instagram** — Reels + carousels; strongest all-around for visual/e-commerce.
+- **YouTube** — long-form builds authority; Shorts drive subscriber growth.
+- **Facebook** — organic reach ~0.06% for pages (pay-to-play); use for groups,
+  local ads, Marketplace.
+- **Pinterest** — high-quality visual, aspirational/visual products.
+
+## Engagement is the real lever
+
+- Replying to comments consistently raises engagement across platforms (up to
+  +42% on Threads, +30% on LinkedIn per Buffer's 2026 report).
+- Answer DMs < 60 min (business hours), comments < 2 hr.
+- Treat DMs/comments as a product surface, not an afterthought.
+
+## Organic + paid = one system
+
+- Pure organic plateaus; pure paid builds no equity. Use organic to find winners,
+  paid to amplify them, creators to extend reach.
+- **Whitelisted creator content** (ads run from the creator's handle) often
+  outperforms brand creative 2–4x on CTR/CPA on Meta & TikTok.
+- Typical mid-market split: 50% paid to cold, 20% retargeting, 20% creators,
+  10% experimentation. Review quarterly vs blended CAC.
+
+## Measurement (3 layers)
+
+- **Leading**: saves, shares, watch-time, DM volume, comment velocity, branded
+  search. Predicts results 60–90 days out.
+- **Lagging**: follower growth rate, reach %, engagement rate per pillar, top
+  posts by format (report vs 90-day median, ignore viral spikes).
+- **Business**: branded search growth, assisted conversions, social-sourced
+  pipeline, CAC contribution, LTV differential. Report as a range; last-click
+  undervalues social.
+- **Server-side conversion APIs are mandatory** (Meta CAPI, TikTok Events API,
+  LinkedIn Conversions API) — client-side pixels miss 30–60% post iOS 17 / ITP.
+  Run geo-lift/holdout tests at least annually.
+
+## Beginner start
+
+Pick one platform, set one clear goal, post useful content, respond to people,
+measure what resonates, then expand. Progress > perfection.
+
+## Sources
+
+- How to create a great social media strategy in 2026 — HubSpot: https://blog.hubspot.com/marketing/social-media-strategy-for-your-business
+- Your Complete Guide to Social Media Marketing (2026) — Buffer: https://buffer.com/resources/social-media-marketing-guide/
+- 7-Step Social Media Marketing Strategy (2026) — Buffer: https://buffer.com/resources/social-media-marketing-strategy/
+- Social Media Marketing Strategy 2026 — Bear My Brand: https://bearmybrand.com/blog/social-media-marketing-strategy-2026
+- Social Media Marketing: A 2026 Strategy Guide — 2POINT: https://www.2pointagency.com/guides/social-media-marketing-the-complete-2026-strategy-guide/
+- Social Media Strategy Template 2026 — Digital Applied: https://www.digitalapplied.com/blog/social-media-strategy-template-2026-full-framework

diff --git a/marketing-docs/05-paid-ads-ppc.md b/marketing-docs/05-paid-ads-ppc.md
new file mode 100644
index 00000000..67141058
--- /dev/null
+++ b/marketing-docs/05-paid-ads-ppc.md
@@ -0,0 +1,88 @@
+# Paid Ads / PPC (Pay-Per-Click)
+
+Primary focus: **Google Ads** campaign structure & best practices, plus general
+PPC principles. PPC buys targeted traffic fast; it stops when the budget stops,
+so it pairs with (doesn't replace) organic.
+
+## What PPC is
+
+You pay per click (or impression/action) to show ads on search engines, social
+platforms, and display networks. Highest-intent traffic: people searching for
+what you offer. Google Search Ads cited at ~4.4% avg conversion rate; organic
+converts ~14.6% vs 1.7% for outbound.
+
+## Google Ads account structure
+
+Top-down hierarchy:
+
+```
+Account
+└── Campaign        (budget, location, network, settings)
+    └── Ad Group    (theme + related keywords + ads)
+        └── Ads + Keywords
+```
+
+**Best practices**
+
+- Organize as you create; reorganizing later wipes accumulated data/learning.
+- Create a **separate campaign** when you need different budget or location
+  targeting (e.g., shirts vs pants).
+- One campaign can share budget/location across ad groups.
+- Each **ad group = one narrow theme** with tightly related keywords.
+- Mention at least one keyword in the ad headline for relevance.
+- Keep **≥3 ads per ad group** and use optimized ad rotation to let Google pick
+  the best per auction (signals: keyword, search term, device, location).
+
+## Campaign types
+
+Choose by objective:
+
+- **Search** — text ads on search results (high intent).
+- **Display** — image ads on websites.
+- **Video** — video ads on YouTube.
+- **Shopping** — product listings on Google.
+- **Demand Gen** — ads within online feeds.
+- **App** — promote your app.
+- **Performance Max** — find high-value customers across all channels.
+- **Local** — promote physical locations.
+
+## Setup flow
+
+1. Add business info (name, URL; link Business Profile / YouTube).
+2. Choose advertising **objective & goal** → Google recommends campaign type.
+3. Set budget & bidding.
+4. Enter payment details.
+
+## Core PPC mechanics to know
+
+- **Keywords & match types** target searches; use negative keywords to cut waste.
+- **Quality Score** (expected CTR, ad relevance, landing-page experience) drives
+  cost & position — write relevant ads and send to strong landing pages.
+- **Bidding**: manual or automated (e.g., maximize clicks/conversions, tCPA,
+  tROAS). Start simple, then automate once data exists.
+- **Conversion tracking** is mandatory — without it you can't optimize.
+- **Search terms report** → refine keywords/negatives continuously.
+
+## Measurement & targets
+
+- ROAS (return on ad spend), CAC, CPL, CPA, CTR, conversion rate.
+- Rule of thumb: aim for **≥3:1 ROAS** on paid channels.
+- Run paid for fast pipeline; use organic (SEO, email, Business Profile) for
+  compounding CPA.
+
+## General PPC best practices (cross-platform)
+
+- Set a clear budget and track every conversion.
+- Test offers/creative in small batches; scale winners.
+- Align ad → landing page message (consistency lifts conversion).
+- Use retargeting to recapture interested users.
+- Watch frequency to avoid ad fatigue.
+
+## Sources
+
+- Organize your account with ad groups — Google Ads Help: https://support.google.com/google-ads/answer/6372655
+- Google Ads Best Practices: https://support.google.com/google-ads/answer/6154846
+- Create a campaign — Google Ads Help: https://support.google.com/google-ads/answer/6324971
+- Campaigns overview — Google Ads API: https://developers.google.com/google-ads/api/docs/campaigns/overview
+- Perfect Google Ads campaign structure (2026) — LeadsBridge: https://leadsbridge.com/blog/google-ads-campaign-structure/
+- How campaign structure shapes performance — Search Engine Land: https://searchengineland.com/how-campaign-structure-shapes-google-ads-performance-481332

diff --git a/marketing-docs/06-content-marketing.md b/marketing-docs/06-content-marketing.md
new file mode 100644
index 00000000..b818a683
--- /dev/null
+++ b/marketing-docs/06-content-marketing.md
@@ -0,0 +1,98 @@
+# Content Marketing
+
+Content marketing attracts, engages, and converts audiences through genuinely
+useful content (articles, guides, video, infographics) rather than interruptive
+ads. It feeds SEO, social, and email, and compounds over time.
+
+## Definition
+
+A **documented plan** defining what content you'll create, for whom, through which
+channels, and how you'll measure success — with every piece tied to a business
+goal.
+
+## The 6-component framework (CMI / 2026 synthesis)
+
+1. **Strategic foundation** — goals/objectives (awareness, demand gen, pipeline,
+   retention).
+2. **Audience intelligence** — for B2B, map the full **buying committee** (a VP
+   and a CTO have different needs); refresh personas annually.
+3. **Content architecture** — 3–5 **content pillars** (buyer pain areas, not
+   product categories) + topic clusters.
+4. **Production & editorial process** — editorial calendar (themes over time) +
+   content calendar (per piece: keyword, persona, funnel stage, owner, date).
+5. **Distribution & promotion** — plan this *before* writing. Budget 25–35% of
+   content spend on distribution for mature B2B.
+6. **Measurement & analytics** — KPIs before copy.
+
+> Only **47% of B2B marketers have a documented strategy** (CMI, 2025). Only ~3%
+> of your market is buying now; build for the 97% still researching.
+
+## Build it in 9 steps
+
+1. Define goals.
+2. Research audience (CRM data, sales calls, social analytics; map buying committee).
+3. Run a **content audit** (inventory; tag keep/update/consolidate/remove).
+4. Choose a CMS + governance model (who can publish, review, style).
+5. Determine content types & formats.
+6. Map content to the buyer journey (TOFU/MOFU/BOFU).
+7. Build the distribution engine (plan "where will this live?" first).
+8. Add the **AEO layer** (see below).
+9. Implement measurement from day one.
+
+## Funnel-stage content
+
+- **TOFU (Awareness)**: educational blog, explainers, newsletters — identify problems.
+- **MOFU (Consideration)**: guides, comparisons, webinars, case-study lite.
+- **BOFU (Decision)**: customer success stories w/ quantified outcomes, security/
+  compliance docs, implementation roadmaps, competitive displacement, pricing.
+
+## The AEO layer (2026)
+
+~45% of B2B buyers use AI for initial research; 67% prefer rep-free buying.
+Tactics:
+
+- Lead each page with a **direct declarative answer**.
+- Build **entity density** (name specific tools, frameworks, companies).
+- Make **H2 headings answer specific questions**.
+- Treat **FAQ sections as strategic citation assets** for AI engines.
+
+## Distribution (B2B ranked by ICP precision)
+
+Owned media (website, email, LinkedIn) first → niche industry media → webinar
+co-hosting → content syndication → LinkedIn paid → community seeding →
+partnerships. Add AEO as a mandatory layer.
+
+## Repurposing = extraction, not resizing
+
+Mine a long-form source (e.g., a 45-min expert conversation) into: short video
+clips, LinkedIn posts, newsletter snippets, FAQ sections, a blog post. This yields
+**3–5x reach at 20–30% of net-new cost**.
+
+## Measurement (CMI 5-stage model)
+
+1. **See Us** — reach, traffic, AI visibility.
+2. **Connect** — engagement, time on page, email CTR.
+3. **Trust Us** — returning-visitor rate, webinar attendance (most teams skip trust).
+4. **Choose Us** — lead forms, content-assisted conversions.
+5. **Champion Us** — retention, referrals.
+
+Add **human-input measurement** (advisory boards, win/loss interviews) — analytics
+show content was *consumed*; conversations show it *mattered*.
+
+## Common mistakes
+
+- Optimizing for traffic instead of pipeline.
+- Publishing with no distribution plan.
+- Building for one persona in a committee buying process.
+- Ignoring AI search as a distribution channel.
+- Scaling production before proving quality.
+- Measuring only what's easy to measure.
+
+## Sources
+
+- Developing a Content Marketing Strategy — CMI: https://contentmarketinginstitute.com/developing-a-content-marketing-strategy
+- Content Marketing Strategy: How to Build One in 2026 — Marketful: https://marketful.com/content-marketing-strategy
+- Ultimate Guide to a Content Marketing Strategy — Semrush: https://www.semrush.com/blog/content-marketing-strategy-guide/
+- 40+ Content Marketing Best Practices (2026) — Twilio: https://www.twilio.com/en-us/blog/insights/content-marketing-best-practices
+- Content Strategy: What Is It & How to Create One — Neil Patel: https://neilpatel.com/blog/content-strategy-a-development-guide/
+- How to Create a Content Strategy That Drives Results — Harvard: https://online.hbs.edu/blog/post/content-strategy

input.content:
# SEO (Search Engine Optimization)

Google-first reference, based on Google Search Central documentation plus current
2026 guidance (AEO/GEO for AI search).

## What SEO is

The practice of improving your website's content, structure, and authority so it
ranks higher in **unpaid (organic) search results**. One of the highest-ROI
channels because traffic is "free" once rankings are achieved, and it compounds.

## How Google Search works (crawl → index → serve)

- **Googlebot** discovers URLs by crawling links, sitemaps, and redirects.
- Pages are **indexed** (added to the index) if reachable and not blocked.
- Google **serves** relevant results to queries.

Key implication: if Googlebot can't find or understand a page, it can't rank.

## Essentials (Google Search Essentials)

- Create **helpful, reliable, people-first content**.
- Use words people actually search for, and place them naturally on the page.
- Make every page crawlable and indexable.
- Avoid deceptive or manipulative practices (cloaking, hidden text, link schemes).

## Beginner checklist (SEO Starter Guide)

1. **Descriptive page titles & meta descriptions** — unique per page; help users
   see relevance in results and can lift click-through.
2. **Use words people search for** in headings and body copy.
3. **Clear, logical site structure** — link important URLs together with
   descriptive anchor text.
4. **Images**: use descriptive filenames and `alt` text.
5. **Links**: build genuine, relevant links; use `rel="nofollow"` for paid/
   untrusted/required-login links.
6. **Duplicate content**: consolidate with canonical tags; pick one canonical URL.
7. **Mobile-friendly & fast**: Google uses a mobile crawler by default. Core Web
   Vitals matter.
8. **Secure (HTTPS)**: preferred and a ranking/trust signal.
9. **Videos**: provide text context; Googlebot can't read text inside video.

## Developer / technical SEO

- Ensure all pages are reachable by an **anchor link** from another findable page.
- Submit an XML **sitemap** to encourage/smaritize crawling of key pages.
- For JS apps with one HTML page, give **each screen/content piece its own URL**.
- Keep key info in **text, not graphics**; use **semantic HTML**.
- Make content available in the **DOM** (CSS `content` property is not indexed).
- **robots.txt** blocks crawling; `noindex` blocks indexing but allows crawling.
- Use **canonical** tags to manage duplicate URLs.
- Use **301** redirects for permanent moves, **302** for temporary.
- Add **structured data** (schema) to enable rich results.
- Use URL Inspection / Rich Results Test to see how Google views your site.
- Manage crawl budget for very large sites via sitemaps + robots.txt.

## 2026 evolution: AEO & GEO

- **AEO (Answer Engine Optimization)** — get cited by ChatGPT, Perplexity,
  Gemini. Tactics: structured data, topic authority, `llms.txt` directives.
- **GEO (Generative Engine Optimization)** — shape content for AI synthesis:
  clear topic sentences, entity-rich phrasing, strong source-quality signals.
- ~45% of B2B buyers now use AI for initial research. Treat AI engines as a
  distribution channel, not a trend.

## Local SEO

- **Google Business Profile** wins the map pack; critical for service-area
  businesses.
- Build citations + reviews; keep NAP (name/address/phone) consistent.

## Timelines

- Traditional SEO: meaningful results in 3–6 months, compounding gains 6–18 months.
- Google Business Profile map-pack movement: ~3–8 weeks.
- AEO/structured-data citation: ~4–10 weeks.

## Measurement

- Google Search Console (indexing, queries, clicks, Core Web Vitals).
- Google Analytics 4 (engagement, conversions).
- Track rankings, impressions, CTR, organic conversions, and now AI visibility.

## Sources

- SEO Starter Guide — Google Search Central: https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/seo-starter-guide
- Google Search Essentials (guidelines): https://developers.google.com/search/docs/essentials
- SEO documentation hub: https://developers.google.com/search/docs
- SEO Guide for Web Developers: https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/get-started-developers
- Technical SEO techniques: https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/get-started
- SEO Starter Guide makeover (what changed): https://developers.google.com/search/blog/2024/02/ssg-gets-a-makeover
